questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing an architecture that uses password-based authentication with frequent rotation (every 30 days). Users complain and security incidents involve password reuse across systems. Which policy change is MOST CONSISTENT with current best practice per NIST SP 800-63B?

options.B: Remove mandatory periodic rotation unless there is evidence of compromise; instead, require password managers for unique long passwords, check new passwords against breach lists (have-i-been-pwned integration or equivalent), enforce a long minimum length (12-15 characters), allow all characters, and prohibit only demonstrably-common passwords

questions.rationale: NIST SP 800-63B Rev 3 explicitly moved away from mandatory periodic password rotation based on research showing that frequent rotation leads to weaker passwords (users pick predictable patterns and increment) and more reuse across systems. The current best-practice guidance is: (1) do not require periodic rotation unless there is evidence of compromise — rotation should be event-driven, not time-driven; (2) enforce a long minimum length (NIST suggests 8 character minimum, but most mature programs use 12-15); (3) allow all printable characters including spaces and Unicode (no composition rules beyond length); (4) check new passwords against breach lists (HaveIBeenPwned has a public API for this, many commercial providers offer similar services) to reject passwords that have been seen in public breaches; (5) provide password managers to users so they can store unique long passwords for every system; (6) deploy MFA as the actual security control, treating passwords as one factor among several. Option B captures this modern guidance. Option A (7 days) increases the problem. Options C and D are absurd. The CISSP exam tests this because it is a specific, current guidance change from NIST and because many organizations still enforce 30-90 day rotation despite the guidance update.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating an authentication system's response to credential stuffing attacks (automated login attempts using credentials from public breaches). Which control set is MOST EFFECTIVE?

options.B: A layered defense: rate limiting (per-IP, per-account, per-password), CAPTCHA or bot-detection challenges after failed attempts, detection of automated patterns (impossible travel, abnormal user-agent, bulk failures), account lockout with adaptive rather than fixed thresholds, breach password detection (blocking known-compromised passwords at login), and mandatory phishing-resistant MFA to make stolen credentials alone insufficient

questions.rationale: Credential stuffing uses credentials leaked in public breaches to attempt logins at other sites, exploiting password reuse. Because the credentials are valid (at the site they were stolen from) and the usernames are typically email addresses, traditional controls like 'strong passwords' and 'long passwords' do not help — the passwords are already strong; they are just being reused. Effective defenses are layered: (1) rate limiting per IP, per account, and per password combination to slow down automated attacks while allowing legitimate users through; (2) CAPTCHA or bot detection challenges triggered after failed attempts to filter automation; (3) detection of automated patterns including impossible travel, abnormal user agents, and bulk failures from a single source; (4) adaptive account lockout that raises friction on suspicious patterns rather than rigidly locking at a fixed threshold; (5) breach password detection that checks login attempts against known-breached password lists (HaveIBeenPwned, proprietary services) and blocks known-compromised passwords at login; (6) mandatory phishing-resistant MFA, which ensures that even if a stolen password is valid, the attacker cannot authenticate without the second factor. Option B captures this layered approach. Option A is inadequate. Options C and D are too absolute. The CISSP exam tests credential stuffing because it is one of the most common attack patterns and because the defense is structured and testable.

questions.stem: A CISSP is asked about 'OAuth consent phishing' (also called 'illicit consent grants'). Which description is MOST ACCURATE?

options.A: Consent phishing is identical to password phishing
options.B: Consent phishing tricks a user into authorizing a malicious third-party application to access their resources via OAuth. The user is genuinely authenticated and explicitly grants access — the attack bypasses MFA entirely because the user's authentication is legitimate. Defenses include restricting which OAuth apps can be consented to (admin consent governance), auditing granted consents, publisher verification, and user education about the specific pattern
options.C: Consent phishing requires the user's password
options.D: Consent phishing is blocked by MFA

questions.rationale: OAuth consent phishing (also called 'illicit consent grants') is a distinct attack pattern from password phishing. In consent phishing, the attacker registers an OAuth application (typically with a plausible name mimicking a legitimate service) and sends the user a legitimate OAuth authorization URL pointing to the real identity provider. The user authenticates normally at the real IdP — no password is stolen, MFA works correctly — and is then presented with a consent screen asking to authorize the attacker's application to access the user's resources (Microsoft 365 mailbox, Google Drive files, SharePoint documents, etc.). If the user approves, the attacker receives an OAuth token that grants the requested access, which persists independently of the user's password and MFA. This bypasses MFA entirely because the user's authentication was legitimate. The Microsoft Midnight Blizzard 2024 incident involved variations of this pattern. Defenses include: (1) restricting which OAuth apps can be consented to without admin approval (admin consent governance); (2) publisher verification so that only verified publishers can request sensitive scopes; (3) auditing granted consents and revoking anomalies; (4) user education about the specific pattern (different from password phishing); (5) conditional access policies that evaluate OAuth consent requests. Option B captures the attack and defenses. Options A, C, D are wrong.

questions.stem: A CISSP is reviewing a legacy application that stores session tokens in local storage in the browser. A reviewer flags this as a concern. Which is the MOST SIGNIFICANT risk?

options.B: Session tokens in local storage are accessible to any JavaScript running on the page, meaning that XSS vulnerabilities anywhere in the application can extract and exfiltrate session tokens. Session tokens should be stored in httpOnly cookies (with Secure and SameSite attributes) so that they are not accessible to JavaScript; combined with CSP headers and XSS mitigations, this significantly reduces token theft risk
options.C: Local storage is cleared too often
options.D: Local storage is incompatible with mobile

questions.rationale: Browser local storage is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page — there is no same-origin or http-only restriction. This means that any XSS vulnerability anywhere in the application (first-party code, third-party scripts, malicious advertisements, compromised dependencies) can read local storage and exfiltrate its contents. Session tokens stored in local storage are therefore vulnerable to any XSS attack, even ones that would not otherwise be able to capture session state. The correct storage location for session tokens is httpOnly cookies: these are not accessible to JavaScript (the httpOnly flag prevents document.cookie access), they are automatically sent with requests to the domain that set them, and they can be protected with additional attributes (Secure for HTTPS-only, SameSite for CSRF protection, Path and Domain for scope limitation). This defense-in-depth combines with: (1) CSP (Content Security Policy) headers that restrict which scripts can execute; (2) output encoding and input validation to prevent XSS; (3) use of safe template languages that auto-escape output; (4) regular vulnerability scanning and penetration testing. Option B captures the concern. Options A, C, D are wrong. The CISSP exam tests session storage because it is a specific, testable modern application security issue.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating whether to use 'passkeys' (synced FIDO2 credentials across devices) for workforce authentication. Which concern is MOST IMPORTANT to address?

options.B: Passkeys provide phishing-resistant authentication with strong user experience, but consumer passkey implementations (Apple iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager, Microsoft) sync credentials across the user's personal devices via the vendor's cloud, meaning the organization cannot fully control the credential's lifecycle. For workforce use, device-bound passkeys (non-syncing, tied to a specific hardware authenticator) provide stronger control, or enterprise-synced passkeys managed by corporate identity providers. The distinction matters for compliance and incident response

questions.rationale: Passkeys are FIDO2 credentials that can (in consumer implementations) be synced across a user's personal devices via the vendor's cloud service — Apple iCloud Keychain syncs across Apple devices, Google Password Manager syncs across Chrome and Android, Microsoft syncs across Windows and Microsoft accounts. This consumer-friendly model provides phishing resistance plus strong user experience (no need to re-enroll a hardware key on every device), but introduces enterprise considerations: (1) the corporate organization cannot fully control the credential's lifecycle when it is managed by a third-party consumer cloud; (2) compromise of the consumer account (iCloud, Google, Microsoft personal) could expose synced passkeys across all the user's devices; (3) deprovisioning on employee termination is harder when the credential is managed by the user's personal cloud; (4) compliance regimes that require strong authenticator control may not accept consumer-synced passkeys. The alternatives for workforce use are: device-bound passkeys (non-syncing, tied to a specific hardware authenticator or platform authenticator) which give the organization full control; or enterprise-synced passkeys managed by corporate identity providers (Microsoft Entra ID with authentication methods policies, Google Workspace with passkey enrollment controls). Option B captures this distinction.

questions.stem: A CISSP is designing access control for a new cloud database. Which model aligns BEST with modern cloud IAM practices?

options.B: RBAC combined with ABAC: start with broad role-based permissions and refine with attribute-based conditions (data classification, resource tags, subject attributes, time and context). Cloud IAM (AWS IAM, Azure RBAC, GCP IAM) natively supports this pattern through policies that combine role assignments with attribute-based conditions

questions.rationale: Modern cloud IAM platforms combine RBAC and ABAC natively. The pattern is: define broad roles at the service level (read access to databases, write access to object storage, admin access to IAM), then refine with attribute-based conditions that check subject attributes, resource tags, request context, and environment. In AWS IAM, this appears as IAM policies with Condition blocks that check tags, source IPs, MFA state, time, and other attributes. In Azure, this appears as RBAC with ABAC conditions. In GCP, it appears as IAM conditions. The combination gives you the simplicity of RBAC (coarse-grained structure, easy to audit) plus the flexibility of ABAC (per-request evaluation of context-specific rules), without requiring a separate policy engine. The mature pattern is: (1) define broad roles for functional purposes; (2) assign users and service accounts to roles; (3) refine access with attribute-based conditions on specific resources; (4) use resource tags as the primary attribute mechanism for classification-driven access. Option B captures this modern hybrid approach. Option A (DAC only) is not how cloud IAM works natively. Option C (MAC only) is too rigid for most cloud scenarios. Option D is not an option. The CISSP exam tests cloud IAM because it is the dominant access control platform for modern workloads and because the RBAC+ABAC combination is specific and testable.

questions.stem: A CISSP is evaluating a design where an application uses impersonation to perform actions on behalf of users. The application stores the user's credentials to log in as them. Which is the MOST SIGNIFICANT concern?

options.B: Storing user credentials to impersonate them is a severe anti-pattern: it violates the principle that credentials should never be shared, creates a high-value target (the credential store), prevents accountability (actions cannot be cleanly attributed), conflicts with modern IdPs, and is incompatible with MFA. The correct pattern is delegation via OAuth (on-behalf-of flow), service accounts with their own identity, or impersonation APIs that preserve audit attribution to both the impersonator and the impersonated user

questions.rationale: Credential storage for impersonation is one of the worst IAM anti-patterns. The problems compound: (1) credentials should never be shared between parties — the principle underlies every authentication design, and storing them breaks it; (2) the credential store becomes a catastrophic target — compromise reveals credentials for every user; (3) accountability is broken — when the application performs an action, audit logs show the user, but the action may have been initiated by the application without the user's knowledge; (4) modern IdPs with MFA, conditional access, and continuous evaluation cannot function when an application is logging in on the user's behalf; (5) password rotation breaks the application; (6) the design conflicts with the user's right to know who is accessing their resources. The correct patterns are: (1) OAuth on-behalf-of flow (token exchange RFC 8693) where the application requests a token that represents both the application and the user, allowing the downstream service to authorize based on both identities and audit accordingly; (2) service accounts with their own identity for actions that do not logically belong to a specific user; (3) Google Cloud and AWS IAM 'impersonation APIs' that explicitly preserve audit attribution to both the impersonator and the target. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are wrong. The CISSP exam tests this because credential storage is a common but wrong approach.

questions.stem: A CISSP is advising on how to handle privileged access for external consultants who need temporary access to sensitive systems. Which approach is BEST?

options.B: Enforce temporary, time-limited accounts with explicit start and end times, JIT elevation via PAM (credential vaulting and session recording), separate accounts from permanent employee identities, mandatory MFA, narrow scope tailored to the consulting task, automatic deprovisioning at engagement end, and ongoing monitoring of consultant activity with alerts on anomalies

questions.rationale: External consultant access is high-risk: consultants are not employees, their security practices and device hygiene are outside the organization's control, they have limited tenure, and their activity may not be monitored like internal admin activity. The correct approach combines multiple controls: (1) temporary time-limited accounts with explicit start and end times so that access does not persist beyond the engagement; (2) JIT elevation via PAM so that admin privileges are granted per-session rather than permanently; (3) separation from permanent employee identities so that consultant activity is distinguishable in logs and audit; (4) mandatory MFA, ideally phishing-resistant; (5) narrow scope tailored to the specific consulting task (least privilege); (6) automatic deprovisioning at engagement end, triggered by date or by explicit termination event; (7) ongoing monitoring with alerts on anomalies (unusual hours, unusual commands, bulk operations, access outside the approved scope); (8) credential vaulting and session recording to provide accountability. Option B captures this. Options A, C, D are reckless. The CISSP exam tests consultant access because MSP and consultant compromises have been high-profile incident vectors (Kaseya, SolarWinds, various MSP breaches) and because the mature response involves well-known specific controls.
